content:
Consider the matrix $A$ below:

$$ A=\left[\begin{array}{llll} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha & \beta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \gamma \end{array}\right] $$

For which of the following combinations of $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$, is the rank of $A$ at least three?

(i) $\alpha=0$ and $\beta=\gamma \neq 0$

(ii) $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=0$

(iii) $\beta=\gamma=0$ and $\alpha \neq 0$

(iv) $\alpha=\beta=\gamma \neq 0$
explanation:
$$ A=\left[\begin{array}{llll} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha & \beta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \alpha \end{array}\right] $$

Required, $\rho(A) \geq 3$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad \rho(A)=3 \text { or } 4 $$

(i) For $\alpha=0$ and $\beta=\gamma \neq 0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & A \cong\left[\begin{array}{lllc} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \beta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \beta \neq 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \because & R_3=R_4 \\ \Rightarrow & \rho(A)=3 \geq 3 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore$ (i) is true.

(ii) For $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=0$

$$ A \cong\left[\begin{array}{llll} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow \rho(A)=2 \npreceq 3 $$

$\therefore$ (ii) is false.

(iii) For $\beta=\gamma=0$ and $\alpha \neq 0$

$$Then,\, A \cong\left[\begin{array}{llll} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow \rho(A)=3 \geq 3 \quad\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(\because \alpha \neq 0) $$

$\therefore$ (iii) is true.

(iv) For $\alpha=\beta=\gamma \neq 0$

$$ \begin{aligned} Then,\,\,\,& A \cong\left[\begin{array}{llll} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha & \alpha \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \alpha \end{array}\right] \\ & A \cong\left[\begin{array}{llll} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow \rho(A)=4 \geq 3 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore$ (iv) is true.

content:
Let $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{R}^3$ denote the set of real numbers and the three dimensional vector space over it, respectively. The value of $\alpha$ for which the set of vectors

$$ \{ [2 \ -3 \ \alpha], \ [3 \ -1 \ 3], \ [1 \ -5 \ 7] \}$$

does not form a basis of $\mathbb{R}^3$ is _______.
explanation:
To determine the value of $\alpha$ for which the set of vectors:

$$ \{ [2 \ -3 \ \alpha], \ [3 \ -1 \ 3], \ [1 \ -5 \ 7] \} $$

does not form a basis of $\mathbb{R}^3$, we need to check when these vectors are linearly dependent. This happens when the determinant of the matrix formed by these vectors is zero.

First, let's form the matrix with these vectors as its rows:

$$ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 & \alpha \\ 3 & -1 & 3 \\ 1 & -5 & 7 \\ \end{pmatrix} $$

The determinant of this matrix is given by:

$$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -3 & \alpha \\ 3 & -1 & 3 \\ 1 & -5 & 7 \\ \end{vmatrix} =$$

To find the determinant, we will use cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$2 \begin{vmatrix} -1 & 3 \\ -5 & 7 \end{vmatrix} - (-3) \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 7 \end{vmatrix} + \alpha \begin{vmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 1 & -5 \end{vmatrix} $$

Compute each of the 2x2 determinants:

$$\begin{vmatrix} -1 & 3 \\ -5 & 7 \end{vmatrix} = (-1)(7) - (3)(-5) = -7 + 15 = 8 $$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 7 \end{vmatrix} = (3)(7) - (3)(1) = 21 - 3 = 18 $$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 1 & -5 \end{vmatrix} = (3)(-5) - (-1)(1) = -15 + 1 = -14 $$

Now substitute these values back into the cofactor expansion:

$$ 2(8) + 3(18) + \alpha(-14) $$

This simplifies to:

$$ 16 + 54 - 14\alpha $$

For the vectors to be linearly dependent, the determinant must equal zero:

$$ 16 + 54 - 14\alpha = 0 $$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$ 70 - 14\alpha = 0 $$

$$ -14\alpha = -70 $$

$$ \alpha = 5 $$

Therefore, the value of $\alpha$ for which the set of vectors does not form a basis of $\mathbb{R}^3$ is 5.

content: Let the sets of eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix B be $$\{ {\lambda _k}|1 \le k \le n\} $$ and $$\{ {v_k}|1 \le k \le n\} $$, respectively. For any invertible matrix P, the sets of eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix A, where $$B = {P^{ - 1}}AP$$, respectively, are

options.content: $$\{ {\lambda _k}\,\mathrm{det}(A)|1 \le k \le n\} $$ and $$\{ P{v_k}|1 \le k \le n\} $$
$$\{ {\lambda _k}|1 \le k \le n\} $$ and $$\{ P{v_k}|1 \le k \le n\} $$

explanation:
Let's analyze the relationship between the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrices $ B $ and $ A $ given the similarity transformation $ B = P^{-1}AP $.

1. Eigenvalues:

If $ \lambda_k $ is an eigenvalue of $ B $, then there exists a vector $ v_k $ such that:

$$ B v_k = \lambda_k v_k $$

Substituting $ B = P^{-1}AP $ into the equation, we get:

$$ (P^{-1}AP) v_k = \lambda_k v_k $$

Multiplying both sides by $ P $, we obtain:

$$ AP v_k = \lambda_k P v_k $$

Therefore, $ \lambda_k $ is also an eigenvalue of $ A $. This shows the eigenvalues of $ A $ are the same as those of $ B $:

$$ \{ \lambda_k | 1 \le k \le n \} $$.

2. Eigenvectors:

Using the same equations, let $ u_k = P v_k $:

$$ A u_k = A (Pv_k) = P (P^{-1}AP v_k) = P B v_k = P (\lambda_k v_k) = \lambda_k (P v_k) = \lambda_k u_k $$

Therefore, the eigenvectors of $ A $ are of the form $ P v_k $. So the set of eigenvectors for $ A $ is:

$$ \{ P {v_k} | 1 \le k \le n \} $$.

From the analysis, the correct option is:

Option C

$$\{ {\lambda _k}|1 \le k \le n\} $$ and $$\{ P{v_k}|1 \le k \le n\} $$

content:
Consider a system of linear equations Ax = b, where

$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 \hfill & { - \sqrt 2 } \hfill & 3 \hfill \cr { - 1} \hfill & {\sqrt 2 } \hfill & { - 3} \hfill \cr } } \right]$$, $$b = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 \cr 3 \cr } } \right]$$

This system is equations admits __________.
explanation:
Ax = B

$$x - \sqrt 2 y + 3z = 1$$ (Inconsistent)

$$ - x + \sqrt 2 y - 3z = 3 \Rightarrow x - \sqrt 2 y + 3z = - 3 \ne 1$$ (Inconsistent)

$$\therefore$$ Both lines are parallel to each other.

$$\therefore$$ It has no solution.

content: If the vectors (1.0, $$-$$1.0, 2.0), (7.0, 3.0, x) and (2.0, 3.0, 1.0) in R<sup>3</sup> are linearly dependent, the value of x is _______.
explanation:
The three vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$ are

$ \mathbf{a} = (1, -1, 2), \quad \mathbf{b} = (7, 3, x), \quad \mathbf{c} = (2, 3, 1) $

They are linearly dependent, which means

$ \det[\mathbf{a}\ \mathbf{b}\ \mathbf{c}] = 0 $

Step 1: Set up the determinant

$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 7 & 2 \\ -1 & 3 & 3 \\ 2 & x & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 0 $

Step 2: Expand the determinant

Using the first row for expansion:

$ 1 \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ x & 1 \end{vmatrix} - 7 \begin{vmatrix} -1 & 3\\ 2 & 1 \end{vmatrix} + 2 \begin{vmatrix} -1 & 3\\ 2 & x \end{vmatrix} = 0 $

Step 3: Compute 2×2 minors

$ \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 3\\ x & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 3(1) - 3(x) = 3 - 3x $

$ \begin{vmatrix} -1 & 3\\ 2 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (-1)(1) - (3)(2) = -1 - 6 = -7 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} -1 & 3\\ 2 & x \end{vmatrix} = (-1)(x) - (3)(2) = -x - 6 $

Step 4: Substitute back

$ 1(3 - 3x) - 7(-7) + 2(-x - 6) = 0 $

Simplify:

$ 3 - 3x + 49 - 2x - 12 = 0 $

$ (3 + 49 - 12) - (3x + 2x) = 0 $

$ 40 - 5x = 0 $

Step 5: Solve for $x$

$ 5x = 40 \implies x = 8 $

✅ Final Answer:

$ \boxed{x = 8} $

content: If $\mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{1}}, \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{2}} \ldots \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{6}}$ are six vectors in $\mathbb{R}^4$, which one of the statements is FALSE?

options.content: Any four of these vectors form a basis for $\mathbb{R}^4$.
It is not necessary that these vectors span $\mathbb{R}^4$.
If $\left\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_3, \mathbf{v}_5, \mathbf{v}_6\right\}$ spans $\mathbb{R}^4$, then it forms a basis of $\mathbb{R}^4$.

explanation:
Given : $\mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{1}}, \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{2}} \ldots \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{6}}$ are six vectors in $\mathbb{R}^4$.

Four vectors that form a basis for four dimensional space must be linearly independent to each other. Hence, the correct option is (A).

content:
Consider matrix $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ k & {2k} \cr {{k^2} - k} & {{k^2}} \cr } } \right]$$ and

vector $$X = \left[ {\matrix{ {{x_1}} \cr {{x_2}} \cr } } \right]$$.

The number of distinct real values of k for which the equation AX = 0 has infinitely many solutions is _______.
explanation:
Given that,

$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ k & {2k} \cr {{k^2} - k} & {{k^2}} \cr } } \right]$$ and $$X = \left[ {\matrix{ {{x_1}} \cr {{x_2}} \cr } } \right]$$

and AX = 0

Which means it is in the form of Homogenous equation.

According to the question,

the system must have infinitely many solutions.

Which is possible only when

|A| = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\left| {\matrix{ k & {2k} \cr {{k^2} - k} & {{k^2}} \cr } } \right|$$ = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ k3 - 2k(k2 - k) = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ k3 - 2k3 + 2k2 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2k2 - k3 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ k2(2 - k) = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ k = 0, 2

$$ \therefore $$ Number of distinct values of K for infinite number of solutions = 2.

content:
Let M be a real 4 $$ \times $$ 4 matrix. Consider the following statements :

S1: M has 4 linearly independent eigenvectors.

S2: M has 4 distinct eigenvalues.

S3: M is non-singular (invertible).

Which one among the following is TRUE?
explanation:
When eigen values of a matrix are distinct, then it will have linearly independent eigen vectors.

So, S2 implies S1.

content:
Consider the $$5 \times 5$$ matrix $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \cr 5 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \cr 4 & 5 & 1 & 2 & 3 \cr 3 & 4 & 5 & 1 & 2 \cr 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$
It is given that $$A$$ has only one real eigen value. Then the real eigen value of $$A$$ is

The value of $$'k'$$ for which the system has infinitely many solutions is _______.
The maximum value of the determinant among all $$2 \times 2$$ real symmetric matrices with trace $$14$$ is ______.
The determinant of matrix $$A$$ is $$5$$ and the determinant of matrix $$B$$ is $$40.$$ The determinant of  matrix $$AB$$ is _______.

Consider the matrix $${J_6} = \left[ {\matrix{ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \cr 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr } } \right]$$

Which is obtained by reversing the order of the columns of the identity matrix $${{\rm I}_6}$$. Let $$P = {{\rm I}_6} + \alpha \,\,{J_6},$$ where $$\alpha $$ is a non $$-$$ negative real number. The value of $$\alpha $$ for which det $$(P)=0$$ is _______.
For matrices of same dimension $$M,N$$ and scalar $$c,$$ which one of these properties DOES NOT ALWAYS hold ?

$$A$$ real $$\left( {4\,\, \times \,\,4} \right)$$ matrix $$A$$ satisfies the equation $${A^2} = {\rm I},$$ where $${\rm I}$$ is the $$\left( {4\,\, \times \,\,4} \right)$$ identity matrix. The positive eigen value of $$A$$ is _______.
Which one of the following statements is NOT true for a square matrix $$A$$?

options.content: If $$A$$ is upper triangular, the eigenvalues of $$A$$ are the diagonal elements of it
If $$A$$ is real symmetric, the eigenvalues of $$A$$ are always real and positive
If $$A$$ is real , the eigenvalues of $$A$$ and $${A^T}$$ are always the same
If all the principal minors of $$A$$ are positive , all the eigenvalues of $$A$$ are also positive

content: The system of equations $$x+y+z=6,$$ $$x+4y+6z=20,$$ $$x + 4y + \lambda z = \mu $$ has no solution for values of $$\lambda $$ and $$\mu $$ given by
The eigen values of a skew-symmetric matrix are
explanation: Eigenvalues of a skew-symmetric matrix are either zero or pure imaginary.

The system of linear equations,

$$4x + 2y = 7$$ ...... (i)

$$2x + y = 6$$ ........ (ii)

Multiplying 2 in both sides of equation (ii),

$$4x + 2y = 12$$ ..... (iii)

By comparing equation (i) and (iii), its left hand side is equal. So, right hand side should also be equal. But $$7 \ne 12$$.

Thus, given system is inconsistent and no solution exists.

$${[P]_{2 \times 2}} = \left[ {\matrix{ {{P_{11}}} & {{P_{12}}} \cr {{P_{21}}} & {{P_{22}}} \cr } } \right]$$

Let, $$\lambda$$1 and $$\lambda$$2 are two eigen values of P. By the property of square matrices,

$${\lambda _1}{\lambda _2} = \left| P \right|$$

According to question, $${\lambda _1} = 0$$.

So, $$\left| P \right| = 0$$

$${P_{11}}{P_{22}} - {P_{12}}{P_{21}} = 0$$

$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 1 & 1 \cr 1 & { - 1} & 0 \cr 1 & 1 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$

By elementary transformation,

$${R_3} \to {R_3} - {R_1}$$

$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 1 & 1 \cr 1 & { - 1} & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 0 \cr } } \right]$$

$${R_2} \to {R_2} - {R_1}$$

$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 1 & 1 \cr 0 & { - 2} & { - 1} \cr 0 & 0 & 0 \cr } } \right]$$

There exist two non-zero rows.

So, $$\rho (A) = 2$$

We have

$$M = \left[ {\matrix{ 4 & 2 \cr 2 & 4 \cr } } \right]$$

The characteristic equation is given by

$$[M - \lambda I] = \left[ {\matrix{ {4 - \lambda } & 2 \cr 2 & {4 - \lambda } \cr } } \right]$$

The given eigenvector is $$\left[ {\matrix{ {101} \cr {101} \cr } } \right]$$.

$$[M - \lambda I]\widehat X = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left[ {\matrix{ {4 - \lambda } & 2 \cr 2 & {4 - \lambda } \cr } } \right]\left[ {\matrix{ {101} \cr {101} \cr } } \right] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (4 - \lambda )(101) + 2 \times 101 = 0 \Rightarrow 404 - 101\lambda + 202 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 6$$

$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 2 & { - 1} & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 3 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & { - 2} & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & { - 1} & 4 \cr } } \right]$$

Characteristics equation of the matrix A is given as following:

$$\left| {A - \lambda I} \right| = 0$$

$$\left| {\matrix{ {2 - \lambda } & { - 1} & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & {3 - \lambda } & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & { - 2 - \lambda } & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & { - 1} & {4 - \lambda } \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (2 - \lambda )(3 - \lambda )( - 2 - \lambda )(4 - \lambda ) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 2,3, - 2,4$$

content:
The following system of equations
$${{x_1} + {x_2} + {x_3} = 3}$$
$${{x_1} - {x_3} = 0}$$
$${{x_1} - {x_2} + {x_3} = 1}$$ has
The rank of $$\left( {m \times n} \right)$$ matrix $$\left( {m < n} \right)$$ cannot be more then
explanation:
Let A be a matrix with dimension of m $$\times$$ n.

m < n

A is non-square matrix, for non-square matrix rank is given as,

$$\rho$$(A) $$\le$$ min (m, n)

$$\rho$$(A) $$\le$$ m

Thus, rank of matrix A cannot be more than m.

content:
Consider the vectors

$$ a=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \end{array}\right], b=\left[\begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 3 \sqrt{2} \end{array}\right] $$

For real-valued scalar variable $x$, the value of

$$ \min _x\|a x-b\|_2 $$

is___________(rounded off to two decimal places).

$\|\cdot\|_2$ denotes the Euclidean norm, i.e., for $y=\left[\begin{array}{l}y_1 \\ y_2\end{array}\right],\|y\|_2=\sqrt{y_1^2+y_2^2}$.
explanation:
$$ a x-b=\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ x-3 \sqrt{2} \end{array}\right] $$

$$ \left\|\begin{array}{c} x \\ x-3 \sqrt{2} \end{array}\right\|=f(x)=\text { norm } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} f(x) & =\sqrt{x^2+(x-3 \sqrt{2})^2} \\ & =\sqrt{x^2+x^2+18-6 \sqrt{2} x} \\ f(x) & =\sqrt{2 x^2-6 \sqrt{2} x+18} \\Let,\,\,\,\,\,\, g(x) & =2 x^2-6 \sqrt{2} x+18 \\ g^{\prime}(x) & =4 x-6 \sqrt{2}=0 \\ x & =\frac{6 \sqrt{2}}{4}=\frac{3 \sqrt{2}}{2}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \text { Point of minima } & =\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \left.f(x)\right|_{x=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}} & =\sqrt{2\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2-6 \sqrt{2} \times \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}+18} \\ & =\sqrt{9-18+18}=3 \end{aligned} $$

content: Consider the matrix $\begin{bmatrix}1 & k \\ 2 & 1\end{bmatrix}$, where $k$ is a positive real number. Which of the following vectors is/are eigenvector(s) of this matrix?
explanation:
Given : $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & k \\ 2 & 1\end{array}\right]$

The characteristic equation is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & |A-\lambda I|=0 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{cc} (1-\lambda) & k \\ 2 & (1-\lambda) \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & (1-\lambda)^2-2 k=0 \\ & \lambda^2+1-2 \lambda-2 k=0 \\ & \lambda^2-2 \lambda+(1-2 k)=0 \\ & \lambda=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4-4(1-2 k)}}{2} \\ & \lambda=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{8 k}}{2}=1 \pm \sqrt{2 k} \end{aligned} $$

For any eign vector $[X]$ for a matrix $[A]$ corresponding to Eigen value $\lambda$, the following matrix equation satisfies

$$ [A-\lambda I] X=0 $$

For $\lambda=1+\sqrt{2 k}$,

$$ \left[\begin{array}{cc} -\sqrt{2 k} & k \\ 2 & -\sqrt{2 k} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{array}\right]=0 $$

Applying elementary transformation,

$$ R_2 \rightarrow R_1+\sqrt{\frac{k}{2}} R_2 $$

Rank $=1$, Number of variables $=2$

Free variable $=2-1=1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{cc} -\sqrt{2 k} & k \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{array}\right]=0} \\ & -\sqrt{2 k} X_1+k X_2=0 \\ & k X_2=\sqrt{2 k} X_1 \\ & X_2=\sqrt{\frac{2}{k}} X_1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} Let\,\,\,\,\,& X_1=k_1 \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{l} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{array}\right]=k_1\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ \sqrt{\frac{2}{k}} \end{array}\right]} \\ & X=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ \sqrt{\frac{2}{k}} \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} $$

For corresponding, $\lambda=1+\sqrt{2 k}$

So, option (B) is correct.

For $\lambda=1-\sqrt{2 k}$,

$$ \left[\begin{array}{cc} \sqrt{2 k} & k \\ 2 & \sqrt{2 k} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{array}\right]=0 $$

Applying elementary transformation

$$ \begin{aligned} & R_2 \rightarrow R_1-\sqrt{\frac{k}{2}} R_2, \\ & \qquad\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sqrt{2 k} & k \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{array}\right]=0 \\ & \text { Rank }=1 \text {, Number of variables }=2 \\ & \text { Free variables }=2-1=1 \\ & \qquad \sqrt{2 k} X_1+k X_2=0 \\ & k X_2=-\sqrt{2 k} X_1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Let } \begin{aligned} & X_2=-\sqrt{\frac{2}{k}} X_1 \\ & X_1=k_2 \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{l} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{array}\right]=k_2\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ -\sqrt{\frac{2}{k}} \end{array}\right]} \\ & X=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ -\sqrt{\frac{2}{k}} \end{array}\right] \text { for corresponding } \\ & \lambda=1-\sqrt{2 k} \end{aligned} $$

So, option (A) is correct.

Hence, correct options are (A) and (B).

content: Let $$x$$ be an $$n \times 1$$ real column vector with length $$l = \sqrt {{x^T}x} $$. The trace of the matrix $$P = x{x^T}$$ is
explanation:
To determine the trace of the matrix $ P = x{x^T} $, we will follow these steps:

Given that $ x $ is an $ n \times 1 $ real column vector, we have:

$$ l = \sqrt{x^T x} $$

The matrix $ x x^T $ is an $ n \times n $ matrix. Specifically, if $ x $ is given by:

$$ x = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{pmatrix} $$

Then the matrix $ x x^T $ is:

$$ x x^T = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 & x_2 & \cdots & x_n \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} x_{1}^2 & x_1 x_2 & \cdots & x_1 x_n \\ x_2 x_1 & x_{2}^2 & \cdots & x_2 x_n \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ x_n x_1 & x_n x_2 & \cdots & x_{n}^2 \end{pmatrix} $$

The trace of a matrix is the sum of its diagonal elements. For $ x x^T $, the diagonal elements are $ x_{1}^2, x_{2}^2, \ldots, x_{n}^2 $. Hence, the trace of $ x x^T $ is:

$$ \text{trace}(x x^T) = x_{1}^2 + x_{2}^2 + \cdots + x_{n}^2 $$

Recall that $ l = \sqrt{x^T x} $, which means:

$$ l = \sqrt{x_{1}^2 + x_{2}^2 + \cdots + x_{n}^2} $$

Squaring both sides gives:

$$ l^2 = x_{1}^2 + x_{2}^2 + \cdots + x_{n}^2 $$

Therefore, the trace of the matrix $ P = x x^T $ is $ l^2 $. Hence, the correct answer is Option A:

Option A: $ l^2 $

content:
The state equation of a second order system is

$$x(t) = Ax(t),\,\,\,\,x(0)$$ is the initial condition.

Suppose $$\lambda_1$$ and $$\lambda_2$$ are two distinct eigenvalues of A and $$v_1$$ and $$v_2$$ are the corresponding eigenvectors. For constants $$\alpha_1$$ and $$\alpha_2$$, the solution, $$x(t)$$, of the state equation is
explanation:
To find the solution of the given state equation of a second-order system, we start with the differential equation:

$$x(t) = Ax(t).$$

Given that $$\lambda_1$$ and $$\lambda_2$$ are the two distinct eigenvalues of the matrix $$A$$, and $$v_1$$ and $$v_2$$ are the corresponding eigenvectors, the general solution to this differential equation can be written as a linear combination of the eigenvector solutions.

Recall that for a system described by the state equation with matrix $$A$$, the solution can be written as:

$$x(t) = c_1 v_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + c_2 v_2 e^{\lambda_2 t},$$

where $$c_1$$ and $$c_2$$ are constants determined by the initial conditions.

Here, the constants $$\alpha_1$$ and $$\alpha_2$$ play a similar role as $$c_1$$ and $$c_2$$, representing the constants associated with each eigenvalue and eigenvector term. Thus, the correct form of the solution is:

$$x(t) = \alpha_1 v_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + \alpha_2 v_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}.$$

In summation notation, this can be expressed as:

$$x(t) = \sum\limits_{i = 1}^2 \alpha_i e^{\lambda_i t} v_i.$$

Hence, the correct answer is:

Option A: $$\sum\limits_{i = 1}^2 \alpha_i e^{\lambda_i t} v_i.$$

content:
Let $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ two non-zero real numbers and v1, v2 be two non-zero real vectors of size 3 $$\times$$ 1. Suppose that v1 and v2 satisfy $$v_1^T{v_2} = 0$$, $$v_1^T{v_1} = 1$$ and $$v_2^T{v_2} = 1$$. Let A be the 3 $$\times$$ 3 matrix given by :

A = $$\alpha$$v1$$v_1^T$$ + $$\beta$$v2$$v_2^T$$

The eigen values of A are __________.
explanation:
Given, A = $$\alpha$$v1v$$_1^T$$ + $$\beta$$v2v$$_2^T$$

Post multiply by v1 on both sides,

We get,

$$A{v_1} = (\alpha {v_1}v_1^T + \beta {v_2}v_2^T){v_1}$$

$$A{v_1} = \alpha {v_1}v_1^T{v_1} + \beta {v_2}v_2^T{v_1}$$

$$A{v_1} = \alpha {v_1}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ $$\alpha$$ is an eigen value of A.

Post multiply by v2 on both sides,

We get

$$A{v_2} = \alpha {v_1}v_1^T{v_2} + \beta {v_2}v_2^T{v_2}$$

$$A{v_2} = \beta {v_2}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ $$\beta$$ is an eigen value of A.

$${v_1}v_1^T$$ and $${v_2}v_2^T$$ both are singular matrices.

$$\therefore$$ A is also a singular matrix

$$ \Rightarrow \left| A \right| = 0 \Rightarrow {\lambda _A} = 0$$

Hence, $${\lambda _A} = 0$$, $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$

First, we find the characteristic equation of the matrix. This is done by calculating $$|A - \lambda I| = 0$$.

For the given matrix, this equation becomes:
$$\lambda^2 - (4 + x)\lambda + (4x + 9) = 0$$

The question says the matrix has repeated eigenvalues. This only happens if the discriminant of the quadratic equation is zero. The discriminant is $$b^2 - 4ac$$.

Set the discriminant equal to zero: $$(4 + x)^2 - 4 \times 1 \times (4x + 9) = 0$$

Expand the terms: $$(4 + x)^2 = 16 + 8x + x^2$$
$$4 \times 1 \times (4x + 9) = 16x + 36$$

So, $$16 + 8x + x^2 - 16x - 36 = 0$$

Combine like terms: $$x^2 - 8x - 20 = 0$$

Now, factor the quadratic equation: $$(x - 10)(x + 2) = 0$$

This gives $$x = 10$$ or $$x = -2$$

Since the question says x is a positive number, $$x = 10$$.

content:
Consider the following system of linear equations.

$$ x_1+2 x_2=b_1 ; 2 x_1+4 x_2=b_2 ; 3 x_1+7 x_2=b_3 ; 3 x_1+9 x_2=b_4 $$

Which one of the following conditions ensures that a solution exists for the above system?
explanation:
Method 1

Given : Linear equations are

$$ \begin{aligned} & x_1+2 x_2=b_1 \\ & 2 x_1+4 x_2=b_2 \\ & 3 x_1+7 x_2=b_3 \\ & 3 x_1+9 x_2=b_4 \end{aligned} $$

Now, augmented matrix can be written as,

$$ [A: B]=\left[\begin{array}{llll} 1 & 2 & : & b_1 \\ 2 & 4 & : & b_2 \\ 3 & 7 & : & b_3 \\ 3 & 9 & : & b_4 \end{array}\right] $$

Applying $R_2 \rightarrow R_2-2 R_1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & R_3 \rightarrow R_3-3 R_1 \\ & R_4 \rightarrow R_4-3 R_1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \left[\begin{array}{cc:c} 1 & 2 & : \\ 0 & 0 & : \\ 0 & 1 & : \\ 0 & 3 & : \\ b_2-2 b_1 \\ b_3-3 b_1 \\ b_4-3 b_1 \end{array}\right] $$

Applying $R_4 \rightarrow R_4-3 R_3$

$$ \left[\begin{array}{cc:c} 1 & 2: & b_1 \\ 0 & 0: & b_2-2 b_1 \\ 0 & 1: & b_3-3 b_1 \\ 0 & 0 & : \\ b_4+6 b_1-3 b_3 \end{array}\right] $$

For solution to exist

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \rho(A)=\rho(A: B)=2 \\ \therefore & b_2=2 b_1 ; 6 b_1-3 b_3+b_4=0 \end{array} $$

Hence the correct option is (C).

   1 & { - 1} & 0 & 0 & 0  \cr 
   0 & 0 & 1 & { - 1} & 0  \cr

 } } \right]$$ is __________.

The matrix $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ a & 0 & 3 & 7 \cr 2 & 5 & 1 & 3 \cr 0 & 0 & 2 & 4 \cr 0 & 0 & 0 & b \cr } } \right]$$ has det
$$(A)=100$$ and trace $$(A)=14.$$ The value of $$\left| {a - b} \right|$$ is ___________.

If the vectors $${e_1} = \left( {1,0,2} \right),\,{e_2} = \left( {0,1,0} \right)$$ and $${e_3} = \left( { - 2,0,1} \right)$$ form an orthogonal basis of the three dimensional real space $${R^3},$$ then the vectors $$u = \left( {4,3, - 3} \right) \in {R^3}$$ can be expressed as

explanation:
$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ { - 1} & 3 & 5 \cr { - 3} & { - 1} & 6 \cr 0 & 0 & 3 \cr } } \right]$$

The characteristic equation is given by,

$$\left| {A - \lambda I} \right| = 0$$

$$\left| {\matrix{ { - 1} & 3 & 5 \cr { - 3} & { - 1} & 6 \cr 0 & 0 & 3 \cr } } \right| - \left| {\matrix{ \lambda & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & \lambda & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & \lambda \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$\left| {\matrix{ { - 1 - \lambda } & 3 & 5 \cr { - 3} & { - 1 - \lambda } & 6 \cr 0 & 0 & {3 - \lambda } \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$(\lambda - 3)({\lambda ^2} + 2\lambda + 10) = 10$$

$$\lambda = 3$$

$$\lambda = {{ - 2 \pm \sqrt {4 - 40} } \over 2}$$

$$\lambda = {{ - 2 \pm j6} \over 2} = - 1 \pm j3$$

Thus, the eigen value of the matrix are +3, $$-$$1 + j3, $$-$$1 $$-$$ j3 respectively.

The eigen values and the correspondinng eigen vectors of a $$2 \times 2$$ matrix are given by

Eigen value
$${\lambda _1} = 8$$
$${\lambda _2} = 4$$

Eigen vector
$${V_1} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 \cr 1 \cr } } \right]$$
$${V_2} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 \cr -1 \cr } } \right]$$

The matrix is
explanation:
According to the property of eigenvalues, sum of diagonal elements should be equal to sum of values of $$\lambda$$.

So, $$\sum {{\lambda _i} = {\lambda _1} + {\lambda _2} = 8 + 4 = 12} $$ = Trace (A)

Hence, trace (A) = 12. Only option (a) satisfies the property.

A is an orthogonal matrix. (Given)

By the properties of orthogonal matrices,

$$A{A^T} = I$$ (Identity matrix)

$$A{A^T} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \cr } } \right] = I$$

Thus, $${(A{A^T})^{ - 1}} = {[I]^{ - 1}} = I$$

$${(A{A^T})^{ - 1}} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$

First, find the eigenvalues of $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ { - 4} & 2 \cr 4 & 3 \cr } } \right]$$.

The characteristic equation is given by $$\left| {A - \lambda I} \right| = 0$$.

$$ \Rightarrow \left| {\matrix{ { - 4 - \lambda } & 2 \cr 4 & {3 - \lambda } \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow ( - 4 - \lambda )(3 - \lambda ) - 8 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\lambda ^2} + \lambda - 20 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (\lambda + 5)(\lambda - 4) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\lambda _1} = - 5$$ and $${\lambda _2} = 4$$

For any eigen vector [X] for a matrix [A] corresponding to eigen value $$\lambda$$, the following matrix equation satisfies,

$$[A - \lambda I][X] = 0$$

For, $${\lambda _1} = 4$$,

$$\left[ {\matrix{ { - 8} & 2 \cr 4 & { - 1} \cr } } \right]\left[ {\matrix{ {{x_1}} \cr {{x_2}} \cr } } \right] = 0$$

$$ - 8{x_1} + 2{x_2} = 0$$

$$8{x_1} = 2{x_2}$$

Let $${x_1} = K$$

$${x_2} = 4{x_1} = 4K$$

$$\left[ {\matrix{ {{x_1}} \cr {{x_2}} \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 \cr 4 \cr } } \right]K$$

Thus, $$x = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 \cr 4 \cr } } \right]$$ is an eigen vector corresponding to the eigen value $$\lambda = 4$$.

For $${\lambda _2} = - 5$$,

$$\left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 2 \cr 4 & 8 \cr } } \right]\left[ {\matrix{ {{x_1}} \cr {{x_2}} \cr } } \right] = 0$$

$${x_1} + 2{x_2} = 0$$

$${x_1} = - 2{x_2}$$

Let $${x_1} = 2K$$,

$${x_2} = {{ - {x_1}} \over 2} = {{ - 2K} \over 2}$$

$$\left[ {\matrix{ {{x_1}} \cr {{x_2}} \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ 2 \cr { - 1} \cr } } \right]K$$

Thus, $$x = \left[ {\matrix{ 2 \cr { - 1} \cr } } \right]$$ is an eigen vector corresponding to the eigen value $$\lambda = - 5$$.

We know that $$[A{A^{ - 1}}] = I$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left[ {\matrix{ 2 & { - 0.1} \cr 0 & 3 \cr } } \right]\left[ {\matrix{ {1/2} & a \cr 0 & b \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 0 \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2a - 0.1b} \cr 0 & {3b} \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 0 \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2a - 0.1b = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0.1b/2$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3b = 1 \Rightarrow b = {1 \over 3}$$

Now substituting b in equation (1), we get

$$a = {1 \over {60}}$$

So, $$a + b = {1 \over {60}} + {1 \over 3} = {{1 + 20} \over {60}} = {{21} \over {60}} = {7 \over {20}}$$
